query: is adenosine methylation considered an epigenetic modification in current molecular biology literature

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Epigenetic Mechanism -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, DNA Packaging -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: DNA packaging around histones is directly related to gene expression and is the foundation of the field of epigenetics. The extent to which epigenetics contributes to clinical presentations is still actively being determined, but the present body of evidence suggests that variable gene expression contributes to a range of pathologic processes involved in cancers, neurological disorders, and even consciousness, among many other disease states. [34] [35] [36] The mechanisms by which epigenetic changes contribute to disease are complex but generally are related to either increased or decreased expression of specific genes. Two common mechanisms include acetylation of the histone protein to increase gene expression and methylation of histone lysine residues to decrease gene expression. Additional post-translational modifications to histone proteins that affect gene expression include ubiquitination, phosphorylation, and ADP-ribosylation. [34] Each of these modifications will either strengthen or disrupt the DNA-histone interactions to allow for subsequent changes in accessibility by transcriptional enzymes.

retrieved_docs.title: Pharmacogenomics Overview -- Clinical Significance -- Epigenetic Modifications and Drugs
retrieved_docs.content: The enzyme O 6 -methylguanine-DNA methyltransferase (MGMT) plays a crucial role in repairing DNA damage. The repair function of MGMT is vital for normal cellular processes, and its ability to directly reverse alkylation can contribute to the development of resistance to chemotherapeutic alkylating agents in cancer cells. Examples include temozolomide and dacarbazine, which are alkylators commonly used in the treatment of brain tumors, sarcoma, and lymphoma. The presence of MGMT in cancer cells can reduce the effectiveness of these drugs.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, DNA Packaging -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Important to the field of epigenetics is the fact that the changes associated with disease states are reversible, unlike mutations in the DNA sequence itself. With this in mind, the increased understanding of epigenetics has elucidated the potential to treat certain conditions by preventing or reversing the post-translational modifications to histone proteins. For example, methylation of a tumor suppressor gene or deacetylation of a histone protein associated with an oncogene likely contributes to carcinogenesis, so drugs that inhibit these processes have been trialed as potential cancer treatments. [37] The FDA has already approved multiple drugs for treating myelodysplastic syndrome by inhibiting DNA methyltransferases resulting in a decrease in DNA hypermethylation associated with these cancers. [38] Drugs to reverse post-translational histone modifications to methylation and acetylation status have also been trialed for cancers including melanoma, lung cancer, and colorectal cancer, with results showing some promise though many trials are still ongoing. [39] [40]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Epigenetic Mechanism -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Epigenetics is a promising field of research because of the potential to regulate gene expression without changing the DNA sequence, which may likely cause safety and ethical concerns if performed in humans. The most promising way to treat diseases through epigenetic regulation has been through pharmacology. Previous clinical trials for drugs formulated to block epigenetic modifications associated with cancers have proved successful. The FDA has approved a number of these drugs which target epigenetic regulators to treat various cancers including azacytidine and decitabine for myelodysplastic syndrome, panobinostat for multiple myeloma, and romidepsin for cutaneous T cell lymphoma. [32] More drugs are likely to be approved in the coming years as a number of clinical trials for DNA methylation inhibitors and histone modification inhibitors are underway.

retrieved_docs.title: Pharmacogenomics Overview -- Clinical Significance -- Epigenetic Modifications and Drugs
retrieved_docs.content: Histone modification is a notable example of epigenetic modification. Acetylation or methylation of histones can change the transformation of histones, and alter the exposed domains of the DNA double helix when binding. These changes influence gene transcription and can significantly impact cellular response to drug therapies. For instance, histone deacetylation can lead to chemotherapy resistance by silencing genes related to apoptosis. Additionally, histones can undergo O-GlcNAcylation, where an N -acetylglucosamine (GlcNAc) molecule is added between the glycosyl and the serine or threonine residues of nuclear and cytoplasmic proteins. This modification, mediated by O-GlcNAcase, affects various cellular processes, including gene expression and transcription. [62]

retrieved_docs.title: Carcinogenesis -- Biochemical and Genetic Pathology -- Epigenetics
retrieved_docs.content: Epigenetics is defined as changes in DNA expression that are not controlled by inherited factors. This process is characterized by strict regulation and a degree of plasticity, allowing cells to differentiate. Epigenetic regulation involves the following 6 key processes: Posttranslational histone modifications and histone variants DNA methylation and demethylation ATP-dependent chromatin remodeling complexes Polycomb (PcG) and Trithorax (TrxG) complexes Noncoding RNAs and nuclear dynamics 3-Dimensional genome organization

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Epigenetic Mechanism -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: In addition to histone changes, DNA methylation is an epigenetic mechanism associated with gene silencing when the methylation occurs in CpG islands of promoter sequences. [5] Further, non-coding RNA sequences have shown to play a key role in the regulation of gene expression. [6] These epigenetic modifications can be induced by several factors including age, diet, smoking, stress, and disease state. [7] [8] Epigenetic modifications are reversible, but they rarely remain through generations in humans despite persisting through multiple cycles of cell replication. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Primary Protein Structure -- Mechanism -- Splicing and Pre-mRNA Modifications
retrieved_docs.content: Splicing occurs within the nucleus. Several steps are catalyzed by large (60S) molecules called spliceosomes composed of small ribonucleoproteins (snRNPs) and splicing factors. These enzymes excise the introns from the mRNA transcript while leaving exons in the transcript alone. Spliceosomes shuffle around these exons, depending on the type of protein that needs to be synthesized. After splicing, the 5′ and 3′ tails are modified to help the translation process. At the 5′ end of the pre-mRNA molecule, GTP reacts with the first nucleotide in the triphosphate group on the 5′ ribose's carbon and form a 5′–5′ triphosphate linkage; the nitrogen-7 of guanine is then methylated to form the 5 ′ caps of the mRNA. At the 3′ end, the sequence AAUAAA is cleaved by an endonuclease so that a Poly(A)-polymerase can add the adenylate residues. After all the necessary modifications are made, the mRNA strand is considered mature and is then translocated to the cytoplasm to begin translation.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, DNA Structure -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Although four major nitrogenous bases make up the nucleotides of DNA, other uncommon non-primary or modified bases have been found to exist in nature. [14] The most common modified bases in bacterial genomes are 5-methylcytosine, N6-methyladenine, and N4-methylcytosine. These modifications have been shown to protect DNA from restriction enzymes, which cleave DNA at specific sites. In all eukaryotic genomes, the most common modified base is 5-methylcytosine which is critical in regulating gene expression. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Pharmacogenomics Overview -- Clinical Significance -- Epigenetic Modifications and Drugs
retrieved_docs.content: The role of MGMT methylation as a predictive biomarker was validated in the Stupp trial, which demonstrated a significantly greater survival benefit from adding temozolomide to standard radiotherapy in MGMT-methylated glioblastomas compared to the MGMT-unmethylated group. Subsequent long-term studies have reinforced the ongoing importance of MGMT gene promoter methylation in predicting sensitivity to temozolomide. In glioblastoma, MGMT promoter methylation serves as both a prognostic marker and a predictive indicator of response to alkylating agents. [61]

retrieved_docs.title: Pharmacogenomics Overview -- Clinical Significance -- Post-Transcription Modification and Drug Candidates
retrieved_docs.content: Glycosylation, the enzymatic process by which sugars are attached to proteins or lipids, plays a significant role in modulating epigenetic modifications—heritable changes in gene expression that do not involve alterations to the underlying DNA sequence. O-GlcNAcylation is a notable form of glycosylation that plays a crucial role in epigenetic regulation. Androgen-repressed lncRNA LINC01126 drives the growth of castration-resistant prostate cancer by regulating the switch between O-GlcNAcylation and phosphorylation of the androgen receptor. Antisense oligonucleotides inhibit the growth of castration-resistant prostate cancer cells and have the potential to be developed into a therapeutic drug. [66]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Mutagenesis -- Mechanism -- Endogenous
retrieved_docs.content: Base methylation : S-adenosylmethionine (SAM) is used as a methyl donor during physiologic DNA methylation. At a concentration of 4x10^-5M, SAM can generate over 4,000 methylated base changes per cell per day. The significance of this can be depicted in the methylated products O6-methylguanine and O4-methylamine. These highly mutagenic products result in G:C to A:T and T:A to C:G transition mutations. [10] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Histone Code -- Cellular
retrieved_docs.content: There are different types of modifications that exist, such as methylation and acetylation. In methylation, a methyl group (CH3) gets added to a specific nucleotide sequence. Acetylation involves adding an acetyl group (CH3CO) to a specific nucleotide sequence. These modifications can be either activating or repressing for a particular gene, depending on where the alterations occur.

retrieved_docs.title: Glioblastoma Multiforme -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The proneural and neural subtypes arise near the subventricular zone, mesenchymal and classical subtypes are distal to the subventricular zone, and other gliomas occur in the subcortical white matter. [21] N6-methyladenine (m6A), methylation at the sixth nitrogen atom of adenine and regulated by the writers, erasers, and readers enzymes, accounts for the most common form (80%) of post-transcriptional RNA modification observed in GBM. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, DNA Packaging -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The tight structure of chromatin brings about the problem of accessibility to the DNA by enzymes involved in DNA replication and transcription. Chromatin exists in one of two states: heterochromatin, which is condensed and allows little access by transcription enzymes, and euchromatin, which is loose to allow for interaction with transcription enzymes. The transition between these two states is determined by interactions between the DNA and histone proteins via post-translational modifications to the histone proteins like methylation and acetylation. [6] Methylation generally increases interactions between the DNA and histone, thus suppressing gene expression, whereas acetylation will loosen interactions resulting in greater access by transcription enzymes resulting in increased gene expression. [7] The post-translational modifications to histone proteins underlie the mechanisms of epigenetics, which are defined as alterations to gene expression without changes to the DNA sequence. [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 1468
retrieved_docs.content: S‑adenosylmethionin and S‑adenosylhomocystein are both metabolic precursors that give rise to adenosin, indicating that adenosin is a downstream “child” product of each compound. This relationship underscores the role of S‑adenosylmethionin and S‑adenosylhomocystein in the methylation cycle and purine metabolism, where the conversion of these S‑adenosyl derivatives contributes to the formation of adenosin. Understanding that both S‑adenosylmethionin and S‑adenosylhomocystein generate adenosin helps clarify their interconnected functions in cellular methylation processes and highlights their relevance in clinical contexts involving disorders of methylation, liver function, and nucleotide metabolism.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1895
retrieved_docs.content: S‑adenosylmethionine (S‑adenosylmethionin) and glutamine are both classified as members of the broader chemical group “amino acids and derivatives,” indicating that they share the fundamental structural features of amino acids and related compounds. This categorization reflects their roles in metabolism and biochemistry, as both participate in key physiological pathways typical of amino‑acid‑derived molecules. Recognizing S‑adenosylmethionine and glutamine as amino‑acid derivatives helps clinicians and researchers understand their functions, interactions, and potential therapeutic relevance within the context of amino‑acid metabolism.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 2212
retrieved_docs.content: Alkylating agents are classified under the category of pharmacological effects—molecular mechanisms, indicating that their therapeutic action is understood within this mechanistic framework.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 314
retrieved_docs.content: Amino acids and derivatives comprise several biologically important compounds, including L‑carnitine, S‑adenosylmethionine, and glutamine, each of which is classified as a member of this chemical group. These molecules play key roles in cellular metabolism, energy production, methyl‑group transfer, and nitrogen balance, making them essential for normal physiological function and for the management of various metabolic disorders. In contrast, metreleptin, a recombinant leptin analog used to treat leptin deficiency and related metabolic conditions, does not belong to the amino acids and derivatives category; it is a protein‑based therapeutic rather than an amino‑acid‑derived compound. Recognizing the correct classification of these entities supports accurate understanding of their mechanisms of action and appropriate clinical application.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1445
retrieved_docs.content: S‑adenosylmethionin and S‑adenosylhomocystein are metabolic intermediates that each give rise to adenosin, indicating that adenosin is a downstream product of both compounds. Adenosin, in turn, has a broader relationship with neurotransmitery, meaning that adenosin functions as a neurotransmitter involved in neural signaling. This interconnected pathway highlights how the methylation cycle metabolites S‑adenosylmethionin and S‑adenosylhomocystein contribute to the production of the neuromodulatory molecule adenosin, which plays an important role in neurotransmission and related aspects of patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 2 -- Community 415
retrieved_docs.content: Ganciclovir is identified as a nucleic acid synthesis inhibitor, indicating that its mechanism of action involves disrupting the synthesis of viral nucleic acids. In addition, ganciclovir is classified as a member of the broader pharmacologic group “Nucleosides and nucleotides excl. reverse transcriptase inhibitors,” a category that also comprises the antiviral agents cidofovir and brivudine. This shared classification reflects common structural or functional characteristics among these drugs, despite their distinct individual uses. Brivudine, another member of this nucleoside/nucleotide class, is specifically mapped to the chemical entity bromodeoxyuridin, highlighting its molecular identity. Together, these relationships clarify how ganciclovir, cidofovir, and brivudine are related through their inclusion in the same drug class and, for ganciclovir, its specific role as a nucleic acid synthesis inhibitor, which is essential for understanding their antiviral mechanisms and guiding appropriate patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2740
retrieved_docs.content: PARP inhibitory agents are classified as hypoxanthine derivatives, indicating that they belong to the chemical class of hypoxanthine and function as inhibitors of poly(ADP‑ribose) polymerase. This classification highlights the structural relationship between PARP inhibitory compounds and the hypoxanthine scaffold, which is relevant for understanding their mechanism of action and potential therapeutic applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 1152
retrieved_docs.content: The compound 1‑Methyl‑4‑fenyl‑1,2,3,6‑tetrahydropyridin is classified as a dopaminergic agent that acts at dopamine receptors. L‑carnitine, S‑adenosyl‑L‑methionine (SAMe), betaine and glutamine are members of the broader “amino acids and derivatives” class, and within this hierarchy S‑adenosyl‑homocysteine (SAH) and SAMe are child entities; SAMe further contains adenosine as a subordinate compound. Specific amino‑acid derivatives such as chloromethyl‑ketone amino acids, dihydroxyphenylalanine (L‑DOPA) and levodopa are treated as narrower entities under the amino‑acid umbrella, reflecting their role as specialized precursors of catecholamine neurotransmitters. Glutamine, proline and 6‑aminocaproic acid are identified as individual amino‑acid members (parents) within the family. The “Neurotransmitters & Neuroregulators” category explicitly includes amino acids, acetylcholine and dopaminergic agents, underscoring the fundamental link between amino‑acid metabolism and neurotransmitter synthesis. Adenosine, acetylcholine and dopaminergic agents are all classified as neurotransmitters; SAH serves as a metabolic precursor of adenosine, making adenosine a downstream product. Dopaminergic agents belong to the broader neurotransmitter class and encompass mechanisms such as inhibitory dopamine reuptake, with L‑DOPA highlighted as a specific dopaminergic medication. Additionally, the amino‑acid class is linked to the compound pomaglumetad‑methionil through a mapping relationship, indicating a pharmacological association. Collectively, these relationships depict a network in which amino acids and their derivatives form the structural foundation for neurotransmitter agents, dopaminergic drugs, and related therapeutic compounds, emphasizing their central importance in neurochemical function and drug development.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1231
retrieved_docs.content: Abacavir, an antiretroviral nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor used in HIV treatment, is mapped to dideoxyadenosine, indicating a direct chemical or functional correspondence between the two nucleoside analogues. This mapping highlights the structural similarity of abacavir to dideoxyadenosine, which can be relevant for understanding its mechanism of action, potential cross‑reactivity, and the design of related therapeutic agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2106
retrieved_docs.content: Adenosine and acetylcholine are both classified as neurotransmitters, meaning they serve as chemical messengers that transmit signals across synapses in the nervous system. As neurotransmitters, each plays distinct but complementary roles in modulating neuronal activity, influencing processes such as sleep regulation, cardiovascular function, muscle contraction, and cognitive function. Recognizing that adenosine and acetylcholine share this broader classification helps clinicians understand their contributions to normal physiology and to various disorders, guiding therapeutic strategies that target neurotransmitter pathways for conditions like arrhythmias, neurodegenerative diseases, and sleep disturbances.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 626
retrieved_docs.content: Ganciclovir and cidofovir are both classified as members of the “Nucleosides and nucleotides excl. reverse transcriptase inhibitors” group. Within this class, ganciclovir’s mechanism of action is identified as “Nucleic Acid Synthesis Inhibitors [MoA],” meaning it exerts antiviral effects by inhibiting the synthesis of nucleic acids. By belonging to the same nucleoside/nucleotide category, both ganciclovir and cidofovir share structural and functional properties that target viral replication through disruption of nucleic acid synthesis, a key principle in antiviral drug therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2786
retrieved_docs.content: Amino acids and derivatives constitute a biochemical class that includes several important metabolites such as S‑adenosylmethionin (commonly known as S‑adenosylmethionine), betaine, and glutamin (glutamine). These compounds are directly derived from or closely related to amino acids and serve distinct physiological functions: S‑adenosylmethionin acts as a universal methyl‑group donor in numerous methylation reactions; betaine functions as an osmolyte and methyl donor that helps protect cells from osmotic stress; and glutamin is a key nitrogen carrier that supports protein synthesis, energy production, and acid‑base balance. Although some sources may erroneously list metreleptin as a member of this group, metreleptin is a recombinant leptin analog and does not belong to the amino‑acid‑derived compound class. Correctly distinguishing which substances are true members of the amino acids and derivatives category is essential for accurate biochemical interpretation, therapeutic decision‑making, and patient education.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1902
retrieved_docs.content: L‑karnitin, S‑adenosylmethionin, and glutamin are all classified as members of the broader category “Amino acids and derivatives.” This grouping includes both standard amino acids and related compounds that serve essential roles in metabolism, such as energy transport (L‑karnitin), methyl group donation (S‑adenosylmethionin), and nitrogen balance and protein synthesis (glutamin). Recognizing that these three substances belong to the Amino acids and derivatives class helps clinicians and researchers appreciate their shared biochemical context and informs their use in therapeutic and nutritional strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 281
retrieved_docs.content: Rimantadine is classified as a cyclic amine and a direct‑acting antiviral, placing it within a group of compounds that target viral replication mechanisms. A notable physiologic effect of rimantadine is the alteration of cellular synthetic activity, which can influence both viral production and host cell metabolic processes. Recognizing rimantadine’s membership in the cyclic amine, direct‑acting antiviral class and its capacity to modify cellular synthesis helps clinicians understand its mechanism of action, anticipate potential cellular effects, and optimize its use in antiviral therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 265
retrieved_docs.content: in which amino acids serve as foundational molecules that give rise to or interact with key neurotransmitters such as dopamine, acetylcholine, and adenosine, and from which therapeutic agents like levodopa, karbidopa, and investigational compounds are derived. Understanding these interconnections supports a comprehensive view of neurochemical function and informs the development and use of neuropharmacologic treatments.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 1210
retrieved_docs.content: S‑adenosylmethionine and S‑adenosylhomocysteine are biochemically linked to the broader class of amino acids, with S‑adenosylmethionine explicitly identified as a type of amino acid. Both metabolites generate adenosine as a downstream product, indicating that adenosine is a metabolic “child” of these methyl‑group donors. Adenosine, in turn, has a broader relationship with neurotransmitters, reflecting its role as a neuromodulator; this neurotransmitter connection extends to the entire amino‑acid family, which is also described as having a relationship with neurotransmitters. Within the amino‑acid hierarchy, chloromethyl‑ketone‑containing amino acids constitute a specialized subclass. Proline is highlighted as a prototypical or parent member of the amino‑acid group, underscoring its structural importance within this family. The amino‑acid class also shares a broader biochemical relationship with thiamine (vitamin B1), emphasizing the interplay between amino‑acid metabolism and thiamine‑dependent pathways. Additionally, the entity Tolpa is mapped to the amino‑acid category, indicating its association with amino acids. Collectively, these interconnections illustrate how S‑adenosylmethionine, S‑adenosylhomocysteine, adenosine, various amino‑acid subclasses, and related nutrients are integrated through metabolic pathways, neurotransmitter functions, and nutritional co‑factors, which is essential for understanding cellular signaling and metabolic health.
